title: Claude 101 — Capstone Final
excerpt: The certificate exam: 16 questions mixed across all five modules of Claude 101 — what Claude is, prompting, Projects/Artifacts/Skills, Connectors/Enterprise Search/Research, and the specialized surfaces. The trap-spots from every module return here. Pass at 80% and the foundation is proven, not just covered.

questions.prompt: Claude answers by looking up pre-written answers from a database.
questions.explanation: Claude generates a fresh response every time from learned patterns — it does not retrieve canned answers. That generative ability is why context matters so much.
questions.hint: Composer, not librarian.

questions.prompt: Put the three parts of a strong prompt in order.
items: Define the task (the action you want)
questions.explanation: Stage → Task → Rules: set the scene, say what to do, then constrain how.
questions.hint: Scene, order, rules.

questions.prompt: Which are the four competencies of AI Fluency? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: The four D's: Delegation, Description, Discernment, Diligence. Stage/Task/Rules lives under Description.
questions.hint: Four words, all start with D — not Documentation or Deployment.

questions.prompt: 'Read 50 contracts and write a memo.' Which desktop mode, and why?
options: Cowork — a large autonomous deliverable, judged by job size not topic
questions.explanation: Pick by the size of the job, not the subject. 50 contracts into a memo is a big multi-step deliverable that runs while you do something else — Cowork.
questions.hint: Big autonomous job, even with no code.

questions.prompt: Complete the line: 'Projects store knowledge, skills ___ tasks.'
questions.explanation: Projects = the what (knowledge); Skills = the how (process). 'Projects store knowledge, skills perform tasks.'
questions.hint: Projects hold; Skills do.

questions.prompt: What is an artifact?
options: A standalone interactive output in a window beside the chat — document, code, diagram, mini-app
questions.explanation: Artifacts are standalone, interactive outputs Claude renders in a dedicated window — the output you can edit, use, and share.
questions.hint: The finished object on the side table.

questions.prompt: A project's automatic RAG searches across all of your projects as one global index.
questions.explanation: RAG is automatic AND per-project — scoped to that one knowledge base, not a global cross-project index.
questions.hint: Each project searches its own shelf.

questions.prompt: The protocol behind connectors is described as…
options: MCP — 'USB-C for AI,' one open universal standard
questions.explanation: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the open standard behind connectors — 'USB-C for AI,' one universal interface for any tool.
questions.hint: One universal port.

questions.prompt: Connecting your work email lets Claude read your CEO's inbox too.
questions.explanation: Across connectors and Enterprise Search, Claude only sees what you already have permission to access — your inbox, never your CEO's.
questions.hint: Your keys, not a master key.

questions.prompt: You need today's stock price for one company. Best tool?
options: Web search — a quick fact from one or two sources
questions.explanation: A quick specific fact where speed matters is a web-search job; Research would be overkill.
questions.hint: Don't use the crane for a teaspoon.

questions.prompt: Enterprise Search is best described as…
options: A pre-built Project for your whole organization that searches connected tools and cites sources
A personal project only you can see
A way to delete company data
questions.explanation: Enterprise Search ('Ask {Your Org}') is a pre-built org-wide Project — searches across company tools, synthesizes, and cites sources.
questions.hint: The company-wide Project you didn't build.

questions.prompt: Choose a Claude surface by where the ___ already lives, not the topic.
questions.explanation: One intelligence, many surfaces — pick by where the work already happens.
questions.hint: The thing you're getting done.

questions.prompt: Claude Code runs where?
options: In your terminal, IDE, browser, or Slack
Only in the claude.ai web app
questions.explanation: Claude Code is an agentic coding tool that works in your terminal, IDE, browser, or even Slack.
questions.hint: Where developers already work.

questions.prompt: Claude for Chrome is fully released and fine for high-risk tasks on any site.
questions.explanation: Claude for Chrome is in research preview — best for low-risk tasks on trusted sites; it asks before high-risk actions and blocks some categories by default.
questions.hint: Still a preview.

questions.prompt: What's the single idea that ties the whole course together?
options: Claude generates from context, so every feature is about getting the right context in front of it and judging what comes back
Claude memorizes the internet, so you just ask it anything
The more you pay, the smarter it gets
Prompts don't matter if the model is good
questions.explanation: Because Claude generates rather than retrieves, the context you provide is the whole game — and the delegation–diligence loop keeps you accountable for the output.
questions.hint: One idea: context in, judgment out.

questions.prompt: Per the course, uploading a document to Claude is best described as…
options: A shortcut that gives Claude context to consider in its response
A way to permanently train Claude on your data
Something required before Claude will answer anything
A trick to make Claude respond faster
questions.explanation: Uploads are a context shortcut — Claude parses the file to understand your situation, but does not permanently train on your data.
questions.hint: A shortcut to context, not a training session.

title: Putting It All Together
excerpt: Module 4, testable: use-cases by role and the specialized Claude surfaces — Code, Slack, Excel, PowerPoint, Chrome. 13 recognition questions with hints on, with the trap built in (Claude for Chrome is a research preview, not a fully released anything-goes tool) and the one rule that ties it together: pick the surface by where the work lives.

questions.prompt: Where does the course point you to find step-by-step guides and more use-case inspiration?
questions.explanation: Each role's use-cases link to step-by-step guides in the Use Case Gallery — the place to browse the full collection for your specific work.
questions.hint: A 'gallery' of use cases.

questions.prompt: What is Claude Code, and where does it run?
options: An agentic coding tool that understands your codebase and runs commands — in your terminal, IDE, browser, or Slack
A web-only chatbot for coding questions
questions.explanation: Claude Code is an agentic coding tool: it understands your codebase, executes commands, and handles whole dev workflows in natural language — running in the terminal, IDE, browser, or Slack.
questions.hint: Lives in the terminal, not a web chat.

questions.prompt: What's a standout move Claude in Slack offers beyond summarizing threads?
options: Tag @Claude to hand off a coding task and spin up a Claude Code session from the thread's context
questions.explanation: From a bug report or feature discussion, you can tag @Claude and it spins up a Claude Code session using the surrounding Slack context — the discussion becomes where the work begins.
questions.hint: From bug-report thread straight to code.

questions.prompt: Which problem is Claude for Excel especially built for?
options: Tracing formula errors like #REF! and #VALUE! while preserving formula dependencies
questions.explanation: Claude for Excel is a sidebar that analyzes and modifies spreadsheets by conversation — understanding multi-tab workbooks, preserving formula dependencies, and debugging #REF!/#VALUE!/circular references.
questions.hint: Those spreadsheet error codes.

questions.prompt: When Claude for PowerPoint drafts and restructures a deck, what does it preserve?
options: Your existing template and brand styling
Nothing — it rebuilds from a blank theme
questions.explanation: Claude for PowerPoint drafts, edits, and restructures presentations by conversation while keeping your existing template and brand styling intact.
questions.hint: Your brand stays put.

questions.prompt: Claude for Chrome is fully released and recommended for high-risk tasks like purchasing on any website.
questions.explanation: False — Claude for Chrome is in research preview. Anthropic recommends low-risk tasks on trusted sites; it asks permission before high-risk actions (purchasing, sharing personal data) and blocks some site categories by default.
questions.hint: It's still a preview — keep it low-risk.

questions.prompt: Which site categories does Claude for Chrome block by default? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: By default it blocks certain categories — financial services and adult content — and asks before high-risk actions elsewhere.
questions.hint: Money and NSFW.

questions.concept: Pick surface by where work lives
questions.prompt: You're building and committing software. Which surface fits?
questions.explanation: Software development, codebase navigation, and git workflows are Claude Code's home — terminal, IDE, or browser.
questions.hint: Code → Code.

questions.concept: Pick surface by where work lives
questions.prompt: You need to turn an outline into a slide deck. Which surface fits?
questions.explanation: Slide creation and presentation editing live in Claude for PowerPoint.
questions.hint: Slides → PowerPoint.

questions.concept: Pick surface by where work lives
questions.prompt: Per the summary table, Claude Cowork is best for…
options: Complex, multi-step deliverables — research briefs, document creation, data analysis (desktop, and mobile via Dispatch)
questions.explanation: Cowork handles complex multi-step deliverables (research briefs, document creation, file organization, data analysis), on desktop and on mobile via Dispatch.
questions.hint: Big autonomous deliverables.

questions.concept: Pick surface by where work lives
questions.prompt: The rule for choosing a Claude surface: pick by where the ___ already lives, not the topic.
questions.explanation: One intelligence, many surfaces — choose the surface by where the work already happens (terminal, spreadsheet, slide deck, Slack), not by the subject.
questions.hint: The thing you're trying to get done.

questions.prompt: Which of these are specialized Claude surfaces named in this module? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: The five specialized surfaces are Claude Code, Slack, Excel, PowerPoint, and Chrome. 'Claude for TikTok' isn't a thing.
questions.hint: Five real ones; one invented.

questions.prompt: What is Claude for Chrome?
options: A browser-sidebar extension that observes pages and can take actions in your browser
A new web browser that replaces Chrome
questions.explanation: Claude for Chrome is a browser extension that adds Claude as a sidebar — it can summarize pages, draft emails, automate forms, and hold context across tabs.
questions.hint: A sidebar in the browser you already use.

questions.prompt: What does Claude in Slack let you do?
options: Get help in channels/threads — draft replies, summarize threads, prep for meetings — without leaving Slack
Run financial models in a sidebar
questions.explanation: Claude integrates into Slack for in-context help: drafting replies, summarizing threads, meeting prep, onboarding via channel history — plus handing off coding tasks to Claude Code.
questions.hint: Help inside your team chat.

title: Expand Claude's Reach
excerpt: Module 3, testable: Connectors, Enterprise Search, and Research — the three rings of reach. 15 recognition questions with hints on, with the trap-spots built in (Claude can't read your CEO's inbox, Enterprise Search can't show what you can't access, Research won't run without web search).

questions.prompt: What does a connector let Claude do?
options: Read information and perform actions across your connected apps, from inside the conversation
Permanently store your files on Anthropic's servers
Train a private model on your company data
questions.explanation: Connectors give Claude access to your everyday tools and data so it can search, retrieve, analyze, create, update, and execute tasks across connected apps — turning it from an assistant into an informed collaborator.
questions.hint: A door from the chat into the real tool.

questions.prompt: Connectors are powered by the Model Context ___ (MCP).
questions.explanation: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the open standard behind connectors — described as 'USB-C for AI,' one universal interface for connecting any tool.
questions.hint: The P in MCP.

questions.prompt: The course compares MCP to which everyday thing?
options: USB-C — one universal port that fits everything
questions.explanation: MCP is 'USB-C for AI' — a single universal standard so any developer can build a connector for any tool and it works seamlessly with Claude.
questions.hint: The one cable that replaced all the others.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between a web connector and a desktop extension?
options: Web connectors link cloud services; desktop extensions run locally via the Claude Desktop app for local files and native apps
Web connectors are paid; desktop extensions are free
Web connectors are slower; desktop extensions are faster
There is no difference — they're two names for the same thing
questions.explanation: Web connectors reach cloud services (Drive, Slack, Notion, Stripe…). Desktop extensions run locally through the Claude Desktop app, accessing local files and native applications.
questions.hint: Cloud reaches out; desktop reaches in.

questions.concept: Connector security — sees what you see
questions.prompt: Connecting your work email gives Claude access to your CEO's inbox too.
questions.explanation: Claude sees only what YOU see — it can access data you already have access to, never more. Connecting your work email gives Claude your inbox, not your CEO's.
questions.hint: Your keys, not a master key.

questions.prompt: Where does Anthropic maintain its directory of recommended connectors?
questions.explanation: Browse connectors at claude.ai/directory (Web and Desktop-extension tabs), or click the + button > Connectors in any chat.
questions.hint: A /directory on the main site.

questions.prompt: Enterprise Search is best described as…
options: A pre-built Project for your whole organization — 'Ask {Your Org}' in the sidebar
A way to delete company data
A personal Project only you can see
questions.explanation: Enterprise Search adds 'Ask {Your Org}' to the sidebar — a pre-built Project with your company's knowledge already loaded, searching across connected tools and always citing sources.
questions.hint: The company-wide Project you didn't build.

questions.prompt: Enterprise Search synthesizes across your company's tools and always cites its sources.
questions.explanation: True — it searches across SharePoint, Slack, Gmail, Drive, etc., synthesizes one unified answer, and always cites sources so you can get the full context.
questions.hint: Synthesis you can trace back.

questions.prompt: Put the Enterprise Search setup in order.
items: An admin (Owner) configures it for the org and connects the tools
Each user authenticates their own accounts with the services
Users start asking questions about the org's knowledge
questions.explanation: Two-step: admin configures the org-wide project (required Documents + Chat connectors), then each user authenticates their own accounts, then they ask.
questions.hint: Admin wires the building; then you swipe your own keycard.

questions.prompt: Enterprise Search can show you documents you don't already have permission to access.
questions.explanation: False — it only shows what you already have permission to access in the original tool. Conversations stay private and connected data isn't indexed or stored separately.
questions.hint: Same permission floor as connectors, scaled up.

questions.prompt: How is Research different from a single web search?
options: It runs many searches that build on each other, deciding what to investigate next, then synthesizes a cited report
It searches exactly once and returns the first result
It only searches your own files
It's the same thing with a different name
questions.explanation: Research is agentic — multiple searches that build on each other, pursuing leads and filling gaps, then synthesizing a comprehensive cited report (5–45 min).
questions.hint: A chain of searches, not one.

questions.prompt: Extended thinking is automatically enabled when you use Research.
questions.explanation: True — extended thinking auto-activates so Claude can plan its approach and reason as it gathers, breaking the request into manageable pieces.
questions.hint: Planning needs thinking.

questions.prompt: Research works even if web search is turned off.
questions.explanation: False — web search must be enabled for Research to function. (You can turn web search off only to deliberately do internal-only research across connected tools.)
questions.hint: It needs the web on to reach the web.

questions.prompt: You need today's stock price for one company. Which tool fits best?
options: Web search — a quick, specific fact from one or two sources
Enterprise search — your org's internal knowledge
Extended thinking — pure reasoning, no external info
questions.explanation: A quick specific fact from one or two sources where speed matters is a web-search job. Research would be overkill.
questions.hint: Don't use the heavy crane for a teaspoon.

questions.prompt: You want to know your company's remote-work policy. Which tool fits best?
options: Enterprise search — it lives in your org's internal knowledge
Web search — it's a quick public fact
Research — it needs the open web
Extended thinking — it's a reasoning problem
questions.explanation: A question specific to your company, answered from internal docs/Slack/policies, is an enterprise-search job — not the public web.
questions.hint: Internal knowledge, not the open web.

questions.prompt: Which is the better Research prompt?
options: Analyze the EV battery market — key players, technology trends, and supply-chain challenges affecting investment
Tell me about the EV market
questions.explanation: Be specific about the goal and specify the structure/sections you want (and include constraints). Since Research runs for minutes, a specific, structured prompt pays off.
questions.hint: Specific goal + the sections you want back.

title: Organize Your Work
excerpt: Module 2, testable: Projects, Artifacts, and Skills — the three tools for setting context up once and reusing it. 17 recognition questions with hints on, with the four trap-spots built in on purpose (the description Claude never reads, chats that don't share context, RAG that isn't global, artifacts that aren't search-indexed).

questions.prompt: What best describes a Claude Project?
options: A self-contained workspace with its own knowledge base, instructions, memory, and chats
A single long conversation that never resets
A folder of downloaded files on your computer
A paid plan tier with a larger context window
questions.explanation: A Project is a self-contained workspace — its own knowledge base, custom instructions, memory, and chat histories — for one ongoing work stream. Not a single chat, not a plan tier.
questions.hint: A room you furnish once, not a single conversation.

questions.prompt: Which situation is the BEST reason to create a project rather than just starting a chat?
options: An ongoing workflow with reference materials you'll reuse and consistent requirements
You want Claude to forget previous chats
questions.explanation: Projects pay off for ongoing work — repeated reference materials, consistent requirements, team collaboration. A one-off question doesn't need one.
questions.hint: Ongoing and reusable, not one-and-done.

questions.prompt: The project description you write at setup is read by Claude and steers its responses.
questions.explanation: Claude does NOT see the project description directly — it's there to orient you and your teammates. The steering happens in the project instructions, not the description.
questions.hint: The description is a label for humans; instructions do the steering.

questions.prompt: Which of these do you set when creating a project? Select all that apply.
options: Visibility (private or shared with your org)
questions.explanation: Creating a project is three choices: name, description, and visibility. Model version and budget aren't part of project setup.
questions.hint: Name it, describe it, decide who sees it.

questions.prompt: Two separate chats inside the same project automatically share each other's full conversation context.
questions.explanation: Context is NOT shared across separate chats unless it lives in the knowledge base. The knowledge base is the shared layer; individual chats stay siloed from one another.
questions.hint: Shared shelf, separate desks.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson tell you to give knowledge-base files descriptive names?
options: Claude uses the file name to decide what to retrieve
Claude can't read files with numbers in the name
It's required or the upload fails
questions.explanation: Claude uses filenames (and file proximity) to retrieve the right material — 'Q4-2024-Brand-Guidelines.pdf' beats 'document1.pdf'.
questions.hint: The name is a retrieval cue.

questions.prompt: Which ingredients make up strong project instructions? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Good instructions have four ingredients: context, process, tone/style, and specific requirements. Billing and the description aren't part of them.
questions.hint: Context, process, tone, requirements.

questions.prompt: The course says to think of project instructions as ___ Claude's behavior for the project.
questions.explanation: 'Think of instructions as programming Claude's behavior for this project' — you set standing behavior once instead of asking nicely each chat.
questions.hint: Same verb you'd use for code.

questions.prompt: What does Claude do when a project's knowledge base grows past the context window limit?
options: Automatically switches to RAG, retrieving only the most relevant content
Deletes the oldest files to make room
Asks you to upgrade your plan
questions.explanation: Claude seamlessly enables RAG mode — searching and retrieving only the most relevant pieces instead of loading everything — expanding capacity up to ~10x while keeping quality.
questions.hint: It stops carrying every book and grabs the right few.

questions.prompt: A project's RAG searches across all of your projects as one global index.
questions.explanation: RAG is per-project — scoped to that one knowledge base, and automatic. It is NOT a global index across all your projects. Each project retrieves from its own shelf.
questions.hint: Each shelf is searched on its own.

questions.prompt: Put the three project permission levels in order from least to most control.
items: Owner (controls everything, including who sees it)
questions.explanation: View → Edit → Owner, ascending capability: view is read-only, edit can change instructions/knowledge/members, owner controls everything including access.
questions.hint: Visitor's key, resident's key, owner's key.

questions.prompt: When does Claude automatically create an artifact?
options: When content is significant and self-contained (typically >15 lines) and something you'll edit, reuse, or reference
Every time it writes more than one sentence
Only when you're on a paid plan
questions.explanation: Claude creates an artifact when content is significant, self-contained (usually >15 lines), likely to be edited/reused, stands on its own, and is worth referencing later.
questions.hint: Significant, self-contained, reusable.

questions.prompt: Which of these can Claude render as an artifact? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: All six are named artifact types: documents, code snippets, HTML pages, SVG images, Mermaid diagrams, and React components.
questions.hint: All of them — it's the full list of six.

questions.prompt: When you publish an artifact publicly, what becomes visible to people with the link?
options: Only the selected version of the artifact — your conversation stays private
The artifact and the full chat that produced it
Nothing until they create a Claude account
questions.explanation: Only the selected version becomes public; the chat stays private. Anyone with the link can view, interact, and remix — no account needed.
questions.hint: The painting goes in the gallery, not the sketchbook.

questions.prompt: Published artifacts are indexed by search engines and can appear in Google results.
questions.explanation: Published artifacts are NOT indexed by search engines — they won't show up in a Google search. Public link, but not crawlable.
questions.hint: Public link, but search engines can't find it.

questions.prompt: What is a Skill?
options: A folder of instructions, scripts, and resources Claude loads to perform a specialized task repeatably
A saved chat you can replay
questions.explanation: A Skill is an expertise package — instructions, scripts, and resources Claude loads dynamically to do a specific task the same rigorous way every time.
questions.hint: A recipe card for one job.

questions.prompt: Which everyday capability is actually powered by Skills running behind the scenes?
options: Creating Excel, PowerPoint, Word, and PDF files
questions.explanation: Anthropic's built-in Skills power document creation — Excel, Word, PowerPoint, PDF — invoked automatically when relevant.
questions.hint: The file-makers you've already used.

questions.concept: Skills vs Projects — what vs how
questions.prompt: Complete the course's one-liner: 'Projects store knowledge, skills ___ tasks.'
questions.explanation: 'Projects store knowledge, skills perform tasks.' Projects = the what (information); Skills = the how (process). They complement — a skill can reference a project's knowledge.
questions.hint: Projects hold; Skills _do_.

questions.prompt: Where do you enable Skills, and what must be on first?
options: Settings > Capabilities — with Code execution and file creation enabled
The Projects menu — with sharing enabled
questions.explanation: Settings > Capabilities: make sure Code execution and file creation is on (Skills need the secure sandbox), then toggle individual Skills. It's a preview for Pro, Max, Team, Enterprise.
questions.hint: Capabilities — and Code execution has to be on.

title: The Foundations Ladder
excerpt: Six rungs from the ground up: what data is, what analysis does, and where analytics actually sits. Built and cold-walked in one session.

questions.prompt: At the very bottom of the ladder: what is data?
options: A chart or graph that explains something
Any conclusion you draw from research
questions.explanation: Data = a collection of facts. One fact is a single true thing ("it rained Tuesday"); pile facts together and you have data. A chart is an artifact built FROM data, not data itself.
questions.hint: It's the rawest thing on the ladder — before any chart, conclusion, or insight exists.

questions.prompt: Data analysis turns data into ___.
questions.explanation: Analysis turns data into INSIGHTS — the understanding itself ("long hiring processes cause new hires to quit"). An artifact (a chart, a report) can carry an insight, but the insight is the point. Decisions come after the insight.
questions.hint: An artifact only carries it. The thing itself is the understanding.

questions.prompt: The everyday mechanism of analysis, in order:
questions.explanation: Pattern → Relationship → Prediction → Action. The first move is spotting a PATTERN — not Process (that lives up in the six-step lifecycle). Watch the two-P trap.
questions.hint: Pure Reform Pushes Action. First P = Pattern, NOT Process.

questions.prompt: Data science is the umbrella. Which three disciplines sit under it?
questions.explanation: Data science = "the discipline of making data useful," with three children: Statistics, Machine Learning/AI, and Analytics. (LLMs aren't a co-equal bucket — they're one tool inside ML.)
questions.hint: One of them is the bucket that an LLM lives INSIDE of.

questions.prompt: The three are split by how many decisions you know you want to make up front. Which one is "I don't know yet — I'm exploring"?
questions.explanation: Analytics = you DON'T know how many decisions yet; you're exploring for inspiration, hunting unknown unknowns. The analyst is the explorer/detective. (Statistics = a FEW known decisions; ML = MANY, automated.)
questions.hint: Explorer = don't-know. "Here's data nobody's looked at — go find something."

questions.prompt: In Cassie's split, statistics is for making ___ decisions under uncertainty.
questions.explanation: Statistics = a FEW important decisions under uncertainty, made carefully (its excellence is rigor — the careful philosopher). Many automated decisions = Machine Learning.
questions.hint: The careful philosopher protecting a small number of high-stakes calls.

questions.prompt: In Ask → Prepare → Process → Analyze → Share → Act, which step comes immediately BEFORE Process?
questions.explanation: Prepare comes before Process — you plan (Prepare) before you clean (Process). Your mnemonic encodes it: PUPPIES (Prepare) before PICK (Process).
questions.hint: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally. PUPPIES before PICK.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the Process step (not Analyze)?
options: Clean the data and handle it ethically — consent, restrict access, store securely
Run the data through lenses to find the insights
Share the findings with managers in the right context
Define the business question and what success looks like
questions.explanation: Process = wash and prep: clean the data, get consent, restrict access, aggregate to protect individuals, store securely. Finding the insights is Analyze (the cooking). Defining the question is Ask; sharing is Share.
questions.hint: Food: Process = wash & prep the ingredients. Analyze = cook.

questions.prompt: Diagnostic analytics answers which question?
questions.explanation: Descriptive = what happened · Diagnostic = WHY it happened · Predictive = what will happen · Prescriptive = what should we do. They climb: what → why → what-next → what-to-do.
questions.hint: It's the doctor's word — diagnosing the cause.

questions.prompt: You want the analysis to recommend the best action to take. Which type?
questions.explanation: Prescriptive = what should we do — it prescribes the best action (like a prescription). Predictive only forecasts what will happen; it doesn't tell you what to do about it.
questions.hint: Like a prescription from a doctor — it tells you what to DO.

questions.prompt: In your own words: what does data analysis do to data, and what's the difference between an insight and an artifact?
questions.answer: Data analysis turns data (a collection of facts) into INSIGHTS — the understanding itself, e.g. "long, complicated hiring makes new hires quit." An ARTIFACT (a chart, a report) is just a thing that carries the insight; the insight is the point, the artifact is the delivery.
questions.explanation: The key distinction you sharpened in E27: analysis produces understanding (insight), not just objects (artifacts). Artifacts only carry insights.
questions.hint: One is the understanding itself; the other just carries it.

questions.prompt: From memory, name the six steps of the data analysis process — in order.
questions.answer: Ask → Prepare → Process → Analyze → Share → Act
questions.explanation: The spine of the whole certificate. Prepare before Process (plan before clean); Analyze before Share before Act.
questions.hint: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally.

questions.prompt: Explain what separates statistics, machine learning, and analytics in Cassie's map.
questions.answer: How many decisions you know you want to make before you begin. Statistics = a FEW important decisions under uncertainty (rigor). Machine learning = MANY decisions, automated, under uncertainty (performance). Analytics = you DON'T know how many yet — you're exploring for inspiration (speed). All three sit under the data-science umbrella.
questions.explanation: The dividing axis is the number of decisions known up front — the framing that finally untied the analytics-vs-science knot.
questions.hint: It's about how many decisions you know about up front.

title: The Data Ecosystem
excerpt: Where data lives and the words that get mixed up: the six-verb ecosystem, the cloud, analysis vs. analytics, scientist vs. analyst, and when to trust your gut. Five question types, trap-spots first.

questions.prompt: What is a data ecosystem?
options: The group of elements — hardware, software, and the people who use them — that interact to produce, manage, store, organize, analyze, and share data
A single database where a company keeps all its records
The cloud servers that store a company's data online
The team of data analysts inside a company
questions.explanation: A data ecosystem = various elements (hardware + software tools + the people who use them) interacting to produce, manage, store, organize, analyze, and share data. It's the whole environment data lives in — not one database, not just the cloud, not just people.
questions.hint: Think 'environment data lives in' — tools AND people, doing six things.

questions.prompt: Fill the blank: a data ecosystem's elements interact to produce, manage, store, ___, analyze, and share data.
questions.explanation: Produce · Manage · Store · ORGANIZE · Analyze · Share. Your mnemonic: Penguins Make Soggy Omelettes And Sugar (P-M-S-O-A-S). 'Organize' is the O.
questions.hint: Penguins Make Soggy Omelettes And Sugar — what's the O?

questions.prompt: Select ALL six things a data ecosystem's elements interact to do with data.
questions.explanation: The six: Produce, Manage, Store, Organize, Analyze, Share (P-M-S-O-A-S). Delete, Sell, and Encrypt are not on the list — watch the plausible distractors.
questions.hint: Penguins Make Soggy Omelettes And Sugar — six words, six verbs.

questions.prompt: True or False: 'the cloud' means storing data on your own computer's hard drive.
questions.explanation: False. The cloud is the opposite — data kept ONLINE and accessed over the internet, rather than on a local hard drive or inside your own network. It's a virtual location.
questions.hint: Cloud = online / over the internet, not on your machine.

questions.prompt: Fill the blank: the cloud is a place to keep data ___, rather than on a computer hard drive.
questions.explanation: The cloud keeps data ONLINE — accessed over the internet instead of stored locally. It's just a term for the virtual location.
questions.hint: The opposite of 'on a hard drive' — where data is reached over the internet.

questions.prompt: Which term does this course define as 'the science of data' — the broad umbrella over data, data analysis, and the ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Data ANALYTICS = 'the science of data' — the broad umbrella (in this course's framing) over data, data analysis, and the ecosystem. Data analysis is the hands-on activity inside it. (Cassie's video made data SCIENCE the umbrella — different author; for THIS course, analytics is the umbrella.)
questions.hint: The '-ics' word — the big tent, the science of data.

questions.prompt: True or False: data analysis is the broad umbrella, and data analytics is the hands-on activity.
questions.explanation: False — it's reversed. Data ANALYSIS is the hands-on activity (collect, transform, organize → conclusions). Data ANALYTICS is the umbrella, 'the science of data.' analy-SIS = the step you do; analy-TICS = the big tent.
questions.hint: Which suffix is the small hands-on one? -sis or -tics?

questions.prompt: What's the key difference between a data scientist and a data analyst?
options: Scientists create new questions using data; analysts find answers to existing questions
Analysts create new questions; scientists answer the existing ones
They do the same job under different titles
Scientists clean the data; analysts build the dashboards
questions.explanation: Data scientists CREATE NEW QUESTIONS using data (the explorer — 'what should we even ask?'). Data analysts FIND ANSWERS to existing questions by creating insights. The hinge word on both sides is questions.
questions.hint: One makes the questions, one answers them. Which way round?

questions.prompt: Fill the blank: data scientists create new ___ using data, while analysts find answers to existing ones.
questions.explanation: QUESTIONS — the same word fills both sides. Scientists make new questions; analysts answer existing questions. (Not 'artifacts' — that's the word we killed at E27. Analysis makes insights, not artifacts.)
questions.hint: Same word both sides — and it is NOT 'artifacts.'

questions.prompt: Arrange the six steps of the data analysis process in the correct order.
questions.explanation: Ask → Prepare → Process → Analyze → Share → Act. Mnemonic: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally. Prepare BEFORE Process — you plan/collect before you clean.
questions.hint: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally.

questions.prompt: Data-driven decision-making is defined as using ___ to guide business strategy.
questions.explanation: DDDM = using FACTS to guide business strategy. The other options matter (experience and intuition help) — but the definition itself is fact-led.
questions.hint: The definition's exact word — the thing data is made of.

questions.prompt: Why include subject-matter experts when building data into business strategy? Select all that apply.
options: Their experience and intuition add value to data-driven decisions
They're familiar with the business problem
They can review the results and spot inconsistencies or make sense of gray areas
They are the ones responsible for finding and analyzing the data
questions.explanation: SMEs bring human experience/intuition, know the business problem, and validate results / catch inconsistencies / make sense of gray areas. The last option is the ANALYST'S job, not the SME's — that's the trap.
questions.hint: Three are about business judgment; one is actually the analyst's job.

questions.prompt: True or False: data on its own is more powerful than data combined with human experience and intuition.
questions.explanation: False. The video is explicit: 'data alone will never be as powerful as data combined with human experience, observation, and sometimes even intuition.' Data + people beats data alone.
questions.hint: Data + experience > data alone. Always.

questions.prompt: Fill the blank: gut instinct is an intuitive understanding of something with little or no ___.
questions.explanation: Gut instinct = an intuitive understanding with little or no EXPLANATION — you 'just know,' often picking up signals without realizing it.
questions.hint: You can't ___ why you know it — you just do.

questions.prompt: The 'Data and gut instinct' reading says the right mix of data vs. gut depends mostly on one factor. On a RUSH project with little time, you should lean more on:
options: Thoroughly analyzing all the available data
questions.explanation: The factor is TIME (and resources). Rush / little time → lean more on your own knowledge and experience. Plenty of time + resources → be more data-driven. The ideal is always a blend.
questions.hint: Little time = no time to analyze everything, so you lean on…

title: Meet Claude
excerpt: The whole first module, testable: what Claude is, how to talk to it, and which of the three modes fits the job. 16 recognition questions with hints on — every objective covered, with the Cowork-vs-Chat trap I fell into built in on purpose.

questions.prompt: Claude's design is guided by three principles, often called the three H's. Which set names them?
questions.explanation: Claude is trained to be helpful, harmless, and honest — avoiding toxic/discriminatory outputs, refusing to aid illegal or unethical activity, and behaving truthfully. This is the backbone of its alignment.
questions.hint: Three H's: be useful, do no harm, tell the truth.

questions.prompt: The training approach that aligns Claude with human values and makes it operate transparently is called Constitutional ___.
questions.explanation: Constitutional AI is Anthropic's method for baking the helpful/harmless/honest values into Claude's training so it self-aligns to a set of principles.
questions.hint: A 'constitution' is a set of governing principles — for an artificial what?

questions.prompt: Claude answers by looking up pre-written answers from a database.
questions.explanation: Claude is a thought partner that GENERATES responses from learned patterns — it does not RETRIEVE canned answers from a lookup table. That generative, collaborative ability is what makes it more than a simple chatbot.
questions.hint: Composer, not librarian — it writes the answer, it doesn't fetch it.

questions.prompt: Select every surface through which Lesson 1 says you can access Claude.
questions.explanation: One Claude, many surfaces: web, desktop, and mobile apps, plus Claude Code, Claude in Slack, and Claude for Excel. Excel is the named Office surface — there is no 'Claude for PowerPoint' in the lesson.
questions.hint: Spreadsheets yes, slide decks no.

questions.prompt: In the Claude.ai interface, what does starting a 'new chat' from the sidebar do?
options: Begins a fresh conversation with no prior context
Loads your most recent conversation in full
questions.explanation: The sidebar lets you start new chats or jump back into old ones; a new chat is a fresh context. Projects add persistent context, and Artifacts turn outputs into shareable apps/tools.
questions.hint: New chat = blank slate, no memory of the last thread.

questions.prompt: Put the three parts of a strong prompt in the order the course presents them.
items: Setting the stage (your role, objective, context)
Defining the task (the action you want Claude to take)
questions.explanation: Stage → Task → Rules: first set the scene, then say what to do, then constrain how. The indie-streaming investor-deck prompt models all three.
questions.hint: Set the scene, give the order, lay down the rules.

questions.prompt: Per Lesson 2, uploading a relevant document or image to Claude is best described as…
options: A shortcut that gives Claude context to consider in its response
A way to permanently train Claude on your data
Something required before Claude will answer any question
A trick to make Claude respond faster
questions.explanation: Uploads (PDF, DOCX, CSV, TXT, PNG, JPEG) are a context shortcut — Claude parses the file so it understands your needs, but it does not permanently train on your data.
questions.hint: A file is a shortcut to context, not a training session.

questions.prompt: You want to refine one of your earlier messages rather than pile a new one on top. What does the course tell you to click?
options: The pencil (edit) icon on your message
questions.explanation: Click the pencil icon on any of your messages to edit and resubmit — refining the original prompt instead of stacking corrections on a derailed thread.
questions.hint: Pencil = rewrite the message, don't bolt on a new one.

questions.prompt: Claude's output came back too generic. According to the troubleshooting table, what's the first fix?
options: Add context — audience, role, and constraints
Just tell it to be shorter
questions.explanation: Generic output means Claude lacks context about YOUR situation. The fix is to add audience, role, and constraints — not change models or restart.
questions.hint: Bland output is starved for context — feed it the who and why.

questions.prompt: Select all four competencies of the 4D Framework for AI Fluency.
questions.explanation: The 4Ds are Delegation (what to hand off), Description (how to communicate the work), Discernment (judging the output), and Diligence (using AI responsibly). 'Determination' and 'Deployment' are decoys.
questions.hint: Hand it off, describe it, judge it, own it.

questions.prompt: The Stage / Task / Rules prompt framework lives inside which of the 4D competencies?
questions.explanation: Stage/Task/Rules is about communicating clearly with Claude — defining outputs and guiding the process — which is the Description competency. Discernment is evaluating the result; don't swap them.
questions.hint: Describing what you want = Description.

questions.prompt: Put the four steps of the simple (no-infrastructure) eval approach in the order the course gives them.
items: Gather 5-10 examples of a task you do regularly
Create test prompts that would generate similar outputs
Compare Claude's outputs against your known-good results
Refine the prompts (or flag where human review is essential)
questions.explanation: Evals = systematically testing Claude on tasks that matter to you: gather known examples, write test prompts, compare to known-good, then refine. It's rooted in Discernment.
questions.hint: Gather, prompt, compare, refine.

questions.prompt: In the desktop app, which mode is built for a quick back-and-forth exchange, brainstorm, or draft?
questions.explanation: Chat is the same Claude as claude.ai plus native extras (quick entry, dictation, screenshots) — optimized for quick exchanges and learning via dialogue. Cowork is for sustained deliverables; Code is for building software.
questions.hint: Quick chat = Chat. Big job = Cowork. Software = Code.

questions.prompt: You need Claude to read 50 signed contracts and produce a summary memo of every obligation — a big, autonomous deliverable. Which mode fits best?
questions.explanation: Cowork is agentic: give it a goal, connect resources, let it work through a large multi-source deliverable. The signal is the SIZE of the job, not the topic — it isn't coding, so Code is wrong; it's too big for a quick Chat.
questions.hint: Big autonomous deliverable → Cowork. Size of the job, not the topic.

questions.prompt: Cowork and Code run on the same engine — both are Claude Code underneath.
questions.explanation: Cowork and Code share the Claude Code engine: local, independent work, spinning up sub-agents and sustaining long tasks. Chat is the lighter, conversational surface.
questions.hint: Cowork + Code = one engine (Claude Code). Chat sits apart.

questions.prompt: Which feature set is unique to Code mode?
options: Visual diffs, built-in terminal, git versioning, and local vs. remote dev
questions.explanation: Code adds a full dev environment: Ask/Code/Plan, visual diffs, terminal, git, and local-vs-remote work. Quick entry/dictation/overlay belong to Chat; scheduled tasks and subagents belong to Cowork.
questions.hint: Diffs, terminal, git, local-or-remote = the builder's bench, Code.

questions.prompt: Once you've validated Claude against data you already understand, you've eliminated your responsibility to check its work.
questions.explanation: Validation builds confidence but never removes responsibility. You're still accountable for checking the numbers make sense and being transparent about AI's role — the non-negotiable of the Delegation-Diligence Loop.
questions.hint: Trust is earned, but the buck still stops with you.

questions.prompt: Explain the Delegation-Diligence Loop: why would you test Claude on a task where you ALREADY know the right answer?
questions.answer: Because you have a known-good result to measure Claude against. You pick a task you do regularly, reproduce a past analysis you already completed, and check Claude's output against the answer you already know. If it matches your known result you can trust it for similar future work; if you can't get there after several refinements you've learned this is a task you should not delegate. Validation builds confidence but never removes your responsibility to check the work.
questions.explanation: Testing against a known answer is the only way to measure trust objectively: match = delegate, repeated misses = don't. Rio's veterans-services attendance analysis anchors the idea — you need a concrete, scoped target you can grade against.
questions.hint: Grade Claude on an answer key you already hold — match it to trust it.

title: Claude Code 101
excerpt: The whole Claude Code course, testable: what the agent is, how to drive it with permission modes, the Explore → Plan → Code → Commit workflow, and the four ways to extend it — CLAUDE.md, subagents, skills, MCP, hooks. Recognition questions with hints on, with the trap-spots built in on purpose (it's not ChatGPT for code, Default doesn't auto-accept, /context isn't /compact, and CLAUDE.md guides while hooks enforce).

questions.prompt: What best describes Claude Code?
options: An agentic coding tool that runs its own loop — reading, deciding, acting, verifying — while you supervise
A chat assistant that answers one coding question at a time as you prompt it
A terminal-only autocomplete that suggests the next line of code
A paid model tier that just gives you a larger context window
questions.explanation: Claude Code is an agentic tool: it gathers context, decides its next action, acts, and verifies, chaining steps toward the goal you set. You supervise via approval modes and standing instructions rather than driving each step.
questions.hint: It runs the loop itself — you're the supervisor, not the driver.

questions.prompt: Claude Code is essentially 'ChatGPT for code' — you have to prompt it for each individual action.
questions.explanation: That's the wrong mental model. A chat assistant waits for your next instruction; an agent picks its own next action toward the goal and chains steps (read → edit → test → verify) without you intervening.
questions.hint: Chat waits for the next prompt; an agent chooses the next move.

questions.prompt: Put one pass of the agentic loop in order.
questions.explanation: The loop is gather context → decide the tool → act → verify, then repeat. Each tool result feeds back and informs the next decision.
questions.hint: It ends by checking its own work — then repeats.

questions.prompt: In the agentic loop, which part actually decides what to do next?
questions.explanation: The model's reasoning drives the loop — it decides which tool to invoke and what to make of the result. Tools are passive: they only return information. Remove the model and you have commands with no judgment.
questions.hint: Tools are the hands; something else is the brain.

questions.prompt: Where can Claude Code run? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Claude Code runs in the terminal, VS Code, JetBrains, the desktop app, and the browser (and CI/CD). It's not macOS-only — and every surface shares the same CLAUDE.md and settings.
questions.hint: Many doors in, same house rules — and not one OS only.

questions.prompt: In Default permission mode, what does Claude Code do before editing a file or running a command?
options: Spawns a subagent to do it
questions.explanation: Default is the safest, most transparent mode — Claude asks before every file edit and shell command. You approve, then it keeps executing.
questions.hint: Default is the cautious one, not the fast one.

questions.prompt: Default mode auto-accepts all of Claude's edits without asking.
questions.explanation: The opposite — Default asks before edits and commands. Auto-accept is a separate opt-in mode you switch to with Shift+Tab.
questions.hint: You're thinking of Auto-accept, a different mode.

questions.prompt: What does Plan mode do?
options: Explores and proposes changes without editing anything
Applies all changes, then asks you to review them
Wipes the context window to start fresh
Runs the full test suite before every edit
questions.explanation: In Plan mode Claude reads deeply and proposes a diff for your review without touching disk — the safety checkpoint for complex changes.
questions.hint: Propose first, edit later — nothing hits disk yet.

questions.prompt: Put the daily workflow phases in order.
questions.explanation: Explore (ask questions), Plan (propose a diff for review), Code (implement and verify), Commit (stage, message, PR). Each phase is gated by your review.
questions.hint: Scout, blueprint, build, file the paperwork.

questions.prompt: Which phase holds a concrete proposed diff for you to approve before anything is written to disk?
questions.explanation: Plan is the checkpoint — a proposed change held for approval. Explore is reconnaissance: asking Claude to synthesize answers from the codebase, no diff yet.
questions.hint: Explore scouts; this one draws the blueprint you sign.

questions.prompt: What does the /context command do?
options: Shows what's using space in the context window (read-only)
Summarizes old messages to free up space
questions.explanation: /context is a read-only diagnostic — it reports what's consuming tokens (files, CLAUDE.md, MCP schemas, tool outputs). It doesn't change anything.
questions.hint: It's the fuel gauge, not the fuel burn.

questions.prompt: /context and /compact do essentially the same thing.
questions.explanation: No — /context only reports usage (read-only); /compact actually summarizes older messages to free space. Diagnose with /context, then /compact if you need room.
questions.hint: One reads the gauge; the other burns off weight.

questions.prompt: After you run /compact or /clear, what happens to CLAUDE.md?
options: It's deleted along with the rest of the history
You have to add it back manually
questions.explanation: CLAUDE.md always reloads from disk after compact/clear — it never disappears. If instructions 'vanished,' they were only ever in the chat, which is exactly why standing rules belong in CLAUDE.md.
questions.hint: It's bolted to the project, not floating in the chat.

questions.prompt: You're shipping changes to an authentication flow and want to catch vulnerabilities and data leaks. Which tool fits?
questions.explanation: /security-review is the dedicated audit for threat vectors, data leaks, and misconfigurations — reach for it on auth, payments, or sensitive data. /code-review hunts correctness and efficiency, a different job.
questions.hint: One's a proofreader; you want the locksmith.

questions.prompt: You need the linter to run before every commit — guaranteed, no exceptions. Where does that rule belong?
questions.explanation: Hooks always execute at their lifecycle point regardless of Claude's judgment. CLAUDE.md is guidance Claude tries to follow but isn't guaranteed to. Must-happen-every-time → hook.
questions.hint: Guidance can be skipped; you need a tripwire.

questions.prompt: Hooks execute at their lifecycle point regardless of what Claude decides.
questions.explanation: True — that's the defining property of hooks: deterministic execution. It's the opposite of CLAUDE.md, which is guidance Claude may or may not follow.
questions.hint: Deterministic means no vote.

questions.prompt: How does a Skill differ from CLAUDE.md in how it loads?
options: A Skill loads on demand; CLAUDE.md is always in context
A Skill is always loaded; CLAUDE.md loads on demand
Both load only when you invoke them
Both are always loaded in full every session
questions.explanation: Skills load on demand (by /invoke or auto-detection) at near-zero context cost until used, so they can be long. CLAUDE.md is always-on guidance, so keep it short. Facts/rules → CLAUDE.md; workflows → Skills.
questions.hint: One's a poster always on the wall; one's a recipe card in the drawer.

questions.prompt: What is MCP's job?
options: Connecting Claude to external systems like Jira, Slack, and databases
Packaging an internal repeatable procedure inside your repo
Enforcing a rule at commit time
Summarizing the context window when it fills up
questions.explanation: MCP (Model Context Protocol) gives Claude direct access to external tools and data — Jira, GitHub, Slack, databases. Skills, by contrast, are internal procedures in your repo. MCP = external reach; Skills = internal process.
questions.hint: It's the phone line out to other services.

questions.prompt: What's the main reason to hand work to a subagent?
options: To keep heavy exploration out of your main context — it runs in an isolated window and returns a summary
To permanently store the project's rules and standards
To connect Claude to an external database
To force a command to run on every commit
questions.explanation: A subagent runs in its own isolated context with its own prompt and tools, then returns just a summary — so tasks that would flood your main conversation (deep exploration, dozens of file reads) stay off to the side.
questions.hint: A researcher who reads in another room and brings back one page.
